era_context: Hadrian's reign is in its second decade; the emperor is consolidating the empire and investing heavily in architecture and culture. In China, the Han Dynasty continues under child emperors and regents. The Kushan Empire dominates Central Asia and northern India. In Judaea, Roman rule is established.

events.title: Pantheon in Rome: construction nearing completion
events.description: The Pantheon in Rome, rebuilt under Hadrian's patronage, is nearing completion or completed by 126 CE. The building features a massive concrete dome with an oculus at its apex, representing a pinnacle of Roman architectural and engineering achievement. The Pantheon serves as a temple to all gods and becomes one of the most iconic structures of the Roman Empire. Its construction reflects Hadrian's commitment to monumental architecture and cultural patronage. The building will survive largely intact into the modern era.
events.certainty_note: The Pantheon's construction is well-attested archaeologically. The completion date is debated; some sources suggest completion by 126 CE, others by 128 CE or slightly later.

events.title: Hadrian's Wall construction in Britain: major phase
events.description: Construction of Hadrian's Wall in Britain continues under the emperor's oversight. The wall, begun around 122 CE under the governor Aulus Platorius Nepos, stretches approximately 80 Roman miles (117 km) across northern Britain from the Solway Firth to the Tyne estuary. By 126 CE, major sections are completed or nearing completion, with forts, milecastles, and turrets integrated into the defensive system. The wall serves as a frontier fortification and customs barrier, marking the northern limit of Roman Britain.
key_figures: Aulus Platorius Nepos (Governor of Britain, c. 122–126 CE)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hadrian's Wall excavations and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence and inscribed stones provide strong confirmation of construction phases. The wall's completion date is debated, but major work was underway in 126 CE.

events.title: Han Dynasty: administrative continuity and eunuch influence
events.description: Emperor Shun of Han (r. 125–144 CE) continues to reign as a child emperor, with real power exercised by the imperial harem and increasingly by eunuchs. By 126 CE, the Han bureaucracy continues to function through the examination system and the imperial secretariat, maintaining administrative continuity despite factional struggles at court. Provincial governors report to the capital, and the tax system continues to fund the military and public works. However, corruption and eunuch influence are growing problems that will eventually destabilize the dynasty.

sources.name: Hou Han Shu (History of the Later Han)

events.certainty_note: The broad administrative structure is well-documented, but specific events in 126 CE are not always precisely recorded.

events.title: Kushan Empire: Kanishka I's reign and Buddhist patronage
events.region: Kushan Empire, Central Asia, northern India
events.description: The Kushan Empire, under Kanishka I (r. c. 127–150 CE, though dating is disputed; some scholars place him earlier), continues to flourish as a major power controlling trade routes between China, India, and the Mediterranean. Kanishka is a major patron of Buddhism, commissioning monasteries and supporting the spread of Mahayana Buddhism. His coins, minted in multiple cities, bear inscriptions in Greek, Prakrit, and Kharoshthi, reflecting the empire's multicultural character. The empire's wealth derives from control of the Silk Road trade.
events.certainty_note: Kanishka's reign is well-attested through numismatic and epigraphic evidence, but his exact dates are debated by scholars. The dating c. 127–150 CE is one of several competing chronologies.

disconfirming_evidence: The Pantheon's completion date is debated; some scholars argue it was completed by 126 CE, others by 128 CE or later. Kanishka I's dating remains one of the most disputed issues in Indian and Central Asian history; some scholars place his reign in the 1st century CE, others in the 2nd century CE. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of the events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 126 CE is moderate. Roman sources are relatively abundant but often written decades or centuries after events. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled much later. Indian and Central Asian sources are sparse and often fragmentary. The perspective remains heavily weighted toward elite political and military history, with limited information on rural populations and non-literate societies.

graph_edges.note: Pantheon construction continues and is completed by 128 CE
Hadrian's Wall construction continues through the 120s and 130s
